coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots endowed with selective recognition of the carcinoid tumor biomarkers in biological fluids

coredata.dc:description: Biomarkers are commonly used in both basic research and clinical practice as diagnostic tools. Particularly, cancer biomarkers can be applied to the early detection and diagnosis or screening the tumors. Of special note, malignant tumors can release serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT). In fact, 5-HT is an excellent biomarker for carcinoid tumors since its level in blood plasma is hardly affected by other parameters. Moreover, secretion of 5-HT gives rise to elevated levels of 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (HIAA), the major metabolite of 5-HT, in urine samples of the patients with carcinoid tumors. 5-HT and HIAA are regarded as eligible biomarkers, of which concentrations in blood plasma and urine samples can be utilized for prognosis, and early diagnosis of carcinoid syndrome. Here, we unveil the facile synthesis and characterization of new Tb(III)-doped carbon dots (CDs) in the present work. Strikingly, CDs exhibited superior features for sensing 5-HT and HIAA. It is of worth to note that the Tb(III)-doped CDs induce phosphorescent response to both 5-HT and HIAA in water. Limit of detections (LODs) for 5-HT and HIAA were found to be 0.44 nM and 0.49 nM, respectively. Remarkably, these are the lowest reported values for luminescent systems to date. Moreover, CDs were utilized as chemosensors for 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. To this purpose, extensive luminescence measurements were performed. Accordingly, the obtained results attested that CDs were promising chemosensors for the detection of both 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. Last but not least, we successfully demonstrate that Tb(III)-doped CDs can be used for the detection of 5-HT in artificial cerebrospinal fluid as well as in conditions mimicking the environment within secretory vesicles where 5-HT is stored. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of CDs which can induce remarkable phosphorescent response to 5-HT and HIAA in biological fluids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomarkers are commonly used in both basic research and clinical practice as diagnostic tools. Particularly, cancer biomarkers can be applied to the early detection and diagnosis or screening the tumors. Of special note, malignant tumors can release serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT). In fact, 5-HT is an excellent biomarker for carcinoid tumors since its level in blood plasma is hardly affected by other parameters. Moreover, secretion of 5-HT gives rise to elevated levels of 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (HIAA), the major metabolite of 5-HT, in urine samples of the patients with carcinoid tumors. 5-HT and HIAA are regarded as eligible biomarkers, of which concentrations in blood plasma and urine samples can be utilized for prognosis, and early diagnosis of carcinoid syndrome. Here, we unveil the facile synthesis and characterization of new Tb(III)-doped carbon dots (CDs) in the present work. Strikingly, CDs exhibited superior features for sensing 5-HT and HIAA. It is of worth to note that the Tb(III)-doped CDs induce phosphorescent response to both 5-HT and HIAA in water. Limit of detections (LODs) for 5-HT and HIAA were found to be 0.44 nM and 0.49 nM, respectively. Remarkably, these are the lowest reported values for luminescent systems to date. Moreover, CDs were utilized as chemosensors for 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. To this purpose, extensive luminescence measurements were performed. Accordingly, the obtained results attested that CDs were promising chemosensors for the detection of both 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. Last but not least, we successfully demonstrate that Tb(III)-doped CDs can be used for the detection of 5-HT in artificial cerebrospinal fluid as well as in conditions mimicking the environment within secretory vesicles where 5-HT is stored. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of CDs which can induce remarkable phosphorescent response to 5-HT and HIAA in biological fluids.

Facile synthesis and characterization of new Tb(III)-doped carbon dots (CDs), which exhibited superior features for sensing serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) and 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (HIAA) in biological fluids, are described.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biological markers can be defined as measurable bio-indicators that can effectively describe the characteristics of biological or pathological conditions [1]. Biomarkers are often based on biological molecules that can be measured accurately, sensitively and reproducibly. The measurement or evaluation of biomarkers are generally carried out using body fluids like urine and blood, or tissue samples to investigate the current (normal or abnormal) state of bio-processes, pathogens or pharmacological outcome of a therapy. Hence, they are very useful to screen and foresee the healthy and diseased states as well as intervening the applicable therapy where necessary. In fact, biomarkers are commonly used in both basic research and clinical practice as diagnostic tools since i-they are well characterized, ii-they can truly and repeatedly predict the relevant conditions, and iii-their usability is justified. Particularly, cancer biomarkers can be applied to the early detection and diagnosis or screening the tumors.

Serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine, 5-HT) is a significant neurotransmitter, which modulates mood, appetite, sleep, anxiety, sexuality, learning and memory [2]. Furthermore, it represents a key hormone, which regulates gastrointestinal motility, insulin secretion and glucose metabolism. As such, serotonergic disarray would lead to a variety of pathological conditions: diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular disorders [3]. Critically, there is strong evidence that low levels of 5-HT implicates in the manifestation of mental illnesses such as depressive disorders, anxiety and panic disorders, post-traumatic stress disorder, schizophrenia, eating disorders, attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder, bipolar disorder, obsessive compulsive disorder, and so forth [4]. Moreover, recent work has shown that increased level of 5-HT disrupts autophagy and changes the gut microbiota thereby causing colitis and Crohn's disease [5]. Therefore, real-time detection and monitoring of 5-HT in biological systems may shed light on the biochemistry of 5-HT, thus, discerning its dynamics, as well as bolster deciphering the origins of mental illnesses. Nevertheless, the detection and imaging of 5-HT has proven to be a challenge. Thence, 5-HT has still been a mysterious neurotransmitter; which is viewed as “being involved in everything, but responsible for nothing” [6]. Despite many efforts, limited progress has been made in developing serotonin sensors to date [3,7–11]. The optical sensors developed for neurochemicals are mainly based on engineered proteins [8,9], which can display cross-reactivity with the targeting-drugs. Besides, most of the existing methods to detect 5-HT rely on radiolabeled 5-HT or its analogs lacking the spatial or temporal resolution [9]. Indeed, these methods suffer from limited selectivity, indirect visualization and invasiveness [7]. Thus, the development of new chemosensors for the detection of 5-HT will be welcome.

There is ample evidence on the growth-stimulatory effect of serotonin in carcinoid tumors [12]. Very recently, it was shown that dearth of serotonin would decrease the tumor growth and increase the amount of intratumoural T cells [13]. It is also reported that serotonin is involved in the migration of cancer cells, metastasis and angiogenesis [12,14,15]. Of special note, malignant tumors can release 5-HT. Therefore, 5-HT is present in the blood of cancer patients having malignant tumors. In fact, 5-HT is an excellent biomarker for carcinoid tumors since its level in blood plasma is hardly affected by other parameters [3,10,11,16]. Moreover, secretion of 5-HT gives rise to elevated levels of 5-hydroxyindole-3-acetic acid (HIAA), the major metabolite of 5-HT, in urine samples of the patients with carcinoid tumors [17–19]. 5-HT and HIAA are regarded as eligible biomarkers, of which concentrations in blood plasma and urine samples can be utilized for prognosis, and early diagnosis of carcinoid syndrome [3,10]. Nowadays, mass spectrometry, electrochemical methods and high-performance liquid chromatography are exploited for the detection of 5-HT and HIAA [3,10,20–24]. Unfortunately, these methods suffer from certain drawbacks; they require, i-complex sample preparation, ii-expensive apparatus, iii-toxic reagents, and iv-extensive analysis time. Furthermore, the selectivity and sensitivity of these methods are often reduced in the presence of other neurotransmitters. Taken together, there is an urgent need to develop new chemosensors, which allow selective and sensitive detection of 5-HT and HIAA. In this context, luminescence detection offers a compelling method for real-time sensing and imaging applications with high sensitivity, selectivity, spatial and temporal resolution as well as noninvasiveness. A literature survey identified that there exist only a few reports concerning the detection of 5-HT and HIAA with luminescent nanomaterials [10,25,26]. Wu and Shen have used tetraphenylpyrazine-based luminescent zinc metal-organic framework (Zn-MOF) for turn-off detection of 5-HT and HIAA in DMF-water mixture [3]. Shi and Cheng designed a water stable luminescent Eu(III)-metal-organic framework (Eu-MOF) for turn-off detection of 5-HT and HIAA in water [10]. Very recently, Sun, Zhu and Jiao synthesized a Tb-MOF from 5-sulfoisophthalic acid monosodium salt, and N,N′-piperazine (bismethylenephosphonic acid) by hydrothermal method [10]. The Tb-MOF was used for the turn-off detection of 5-HT and HIAA. However, a shortcoming is that the synthesis and purification of MOFs can be tedious and low yielding. Therefore, there is a growing interest for the development of new platforms which can be used as chemosensors for 5-HT and HIAA. In this context, CDs have remained hitherto unexplored as chemosensors for 5-HT and HIAA.

Carbon nanomaterials have attracted much interest due to their unique electronic and optical features. In particular, carbon dots (CDs) can be considered as relatively new comers to the repertoire of carbon nanomaterials [27,28]. They are easily synthesized from inexpensive materials by simple synthetic methods. Of note, CDs emerged as biocompatible fluorophores. In contrast to organic dyes, in vivo studies showed that CDs are quite stable in the body and hardly metabolized by the cells [29]. Furthermore, CDs are chemically inert and photostable. They can be discharged from the body [29]. Therefore, CDs are promising materials for a variety of applications, including sensing, imaging, drug delivery, and therapy [27,30,31].

In our pursuit to develop novel chemosensors [32–49] we unveil the synthesis and characterization of Tb(III)-doped CDs in the present work [50]. Strikingly, CDs exhibited superior features for sensing 5-HT and HIAA. It is of worth to note that the Tb(III)-doped CDs induce phosphorescent response to both 5-HT and HIAA in water. Moreover, CDs were utilized as chemosensors for 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. To this purpose, extensive luminescence measurements were performed. Accordingly, our results attested that CDs were promising chemosensors for the detection of both 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. Last but not least, we successfully demonstrate that Tb(III)-doped CDs can be used for the detection of 5-HT in artificial cerebrospinal fluid as well as in conditions mimicking the environment within secretory vesicles where 5-HT is stored. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of CDs which can induce remarkable phosphorescent response to 5-HT and HIAA in biological fluids (Chart 1).

Chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and used as received unless otherwise noted. UV–Vis absorption and luminescence spectra were recorded by Varian Cary 50 and Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometers, respectively. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to image the morphology of the samples. Nicolet iS5 FT-IR Spectrometer with iD5-ATR apparatus was used to record the infrared spectra. PHI 5000 VersaProbe, Physical Electronics with a monochromatic Al Kα irradiation source was used for X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The measurement of particle size was carried out with Malvern Nano ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, U.K).

Tb(III)-doped carbon nanoparticles were from a mixture of 1,10-phenanthroline monohydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, 100 mg) and TbCl3.6H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, 63 mg) in ethanol and deionized water (1:1, v/v, 10 mL). This mixture is put in a Teflon-lined container equipped with a stainless-steel jacket and heated up to 160 °C for 5 h. After cooling naturally to 25 °C, CDs were purified with a dialysis bag (3.4 kDa) in water for 24 h before freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence and phosphorescence measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Analyte solutions were 1 × 10−2 M. The concentration of Tb(III)-doped CDs was 1 mg/mL in distilled water. Appropriate amount of this solution was diluted with water at room temperature to get a final solution which was stored at 4 °C.

Quinine was used as standard (Ф = 1.00) for fluorescence quantum yield calculations as follows [51,52].
Φx=Φst (Gradx / Gradst)(η2 x/η2 st)
Φ is the quantum yield, Grad the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript st and x are denoted to standard and Tb(III)-doped CDs respectively.

Artificial blood plasma and synthetic urine samples were prepared according to literature [3,10]. Artificial blood plasma according to PN-EN ISO 10993-15 standard (g/L distilled water) contained NaCl 6.8 g/L, CaCl2 0.200 g/L, KCl 0.4 g/L, MgSO4 0.1 g/L, NaHCO3 2.2 g/L, Na2HPO4 0.126 g/L, NaH2PO4 0.026 g/L as main components each of which was 1.0 mM [53]. On the other hand, synthetic urine consisted of a mixture of 1.0 mM of lactic acid, citric acid, CaCl2, NaHCO3, MgSO4, K2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, NH4Cl, glucose, creatine, creatinine and urea in water [54].

An aCSF solution closely matching the ionic concentrations [1] and physiological compatibility of the CSF was prepared as follows. Two solutions viz. Solution A and Solution B were prepared separately. Solution A was prepared by mixing appropriate amounts of NaCl (8.66 g), KCl (0.224 g), CaCl2 2H2O (0.206 g), and MgCl2 6H2O (0.163 g) in 500 mL ultra-pure water. The solution B was prepared by mixing Na2HPO4 7H2O (0.214 g), and NaH2PO4 H2O (0.027 g) in 500 mL ultra-pure water. These solutions were mixed in a 1:1 ratio to prepare the physiological ion solution of aCSF. The aCSF solution was stored at 4 °C and used for the recovery experiments.

To mimic the environment within secretory vesicles of specialized cells where serotonin is stored [7], 25 mM HEPES solution containing 50 mM Na2S2O3 and 120 mM NaCl was prepared and the pH was adjusted to 5.0. To this solution, CDs were added before the addition of other analytes and the luminescence spectra were recorded.

All the solutions were freshly prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of CDs (1 mg/mL) and analytes (1 mM or 10 mM) in distilled water before ultrasonication for 2 min. Certain amounts of analytes in aqueous solutions were added to suspension of CDs in (3 mL) distilled water, simulated blood plasma, synthetic urine, aCSF or the environment within secretory vesicles for spectrophotometric titrations. The luminescence spectra were recorded immediately in phosphorescence mode in the range of 300–700 nm [40,42,43].

Tb(III)-doped CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method at 160 °C for 5 h, and they were characterized by traditional spectroscopic methods. TEM analysis of Tb(III)-doped CDs showed semi-spherical shape morphology with a good dispersion as depicted in Fig. 1 A. The average particle size of CDs was around 5.5 nm which was based on TEM images. The hydrodynamic diameter of CDs in an aqueous solution was also determined by DLS analysis, which revealed an average particle size of 7.11 nm with a polydispersity index of 0.48 (Fig. S1). XPS spectra was recorded to identify the chemical composition of CDs. The XPS spectrum shows six main peaks at 1241.85 eV, 531.33 eV, 399.09 eV, 285.02 eV, 198.06 eV, and 175.04 eV which corresponds to characteristic peaks of Tb 3d, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, CI 2p, and Tb 4d, respectively (Fig. 1C). The zeta potential (ζ-) of CNDs was +11.4 mV (Fig. S1). Last, the functional group analysis was carried out by using FTIR analysis (Fig. 1D). The peaks at 3361 cm−1, 1618 cm−1, 1140 cm−1 and 728 cm−1 represents stretching and bending vibrations of O–H, CC, CN, C–O and Tb bonds in Tb-CNDs, respectively.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Tb(III)-doped CDs exhibits two peaks at 230 nm and 270 nm assigned for n-π and π-π* transitions (Fig. 2 A). Fluorescence spectrum featured a strong emission band centered at 365 nm when excited at 300 nm (Fig. 2A). Excitation spectrum was monitored at 360 nm. As expected, CDs showed characteristic phosphorescence bands of Tb(III) ions at 491 nm, 546 nm, 585 nm and 623 nm (λex = 305 nm).

The excitation wavelength dependence of luminescence spectra was also examined. It was found that both fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of Tb(III)-doped CDs were independent of excitation wavelength (Fig. 2B and C). The data indicated the presence of uniform emissive centers in CDs with similar energy levels [27,55]. Additionally, narrow emissions indicated that the surface structure and size distribution of CDs were uniform [27]. Using quinine as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φf) of CDs was calculated as 0.015. We also tested the optical stability of CDs using continuous irradiation white LEDs. We have found that CDs were quite stable maintaining more than 98 % of its band intensities even after an hour of irradiation (Fig. 2D). Considering the fact that most of the fluorophores are quickly photobleached within a few minutes [56], the optical stability of CDs was remarkable. The CDs were resistant to photobleaching. Since CDs have a small size, a large specific surface area, and abundant surface functional groups, their optical properties, especially luminescent properties, can be sensitive to the ionic strength of the surrounding environment [27]. Therefore, we assessed the stability of CDs in NaCl solutions. We observed that the ionic strength of NaCl did not affect the optical properties of the CDs (Fig. 2E).

We carried out luminescent titration experiments to investigate the premise of CDs for the recognition of biologically important ions and molecules [2]. A variety of ions (3.33 × 10−6 M) including NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, Bu4NF, Bu4NCl, Bu4NBr, Bu4NI, Bu4NOAc, Bu4NOH, Bu4PF6, Bu4NBF4, Bu4NClO4, Bu4NH2PO4, Na2S, KCN, NaN3, K3PO4, K2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na3PO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaNO3, NaNO2, NaHSO4, Na2SO4, MgSO4, and NH4Cl were added separately to CDs solution. There was almost no change in the phosphorescence spectra of CDs as shown in Fig. 3 A. Furthermore, reactive oxygen species (H2O2, ClO−, 1O2, KO2, tBuO., tBuOOH), bio-phosphates (ATP, ADP, AMP, GTP, GDP, GMP, and PPi), amino acids (GSH, proline, histidine, serine, homocysteine, lysine, isoleucine, cysteine, tyrosine, glycine, histamine) and biologically relevant species (i.e. sarcosine, creatine, creatinine, urea, uric acid, acetone, glucose, lactic acid, citric acid, ascorbic acid, folic acid and dopamine) were also tested (the final concentration of all species were 3.33 × 10−6 M). All of these species caused indiscernible changes in the luminescence spectra of CDs (Fig. 3A). However, addition of 5-HT (3.33 × 10−6 M) induced remarkable changes in the luminescent spectra of CDs (Fig. 3A and B). As shown in Fig. 3B, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs was reduced dramatically upon addition of 5-HT with a quenching efficiency of 75 % [3]. In a similar vein, we also analyzed the response of CDs against HIAA, the major metabolite of 5-HT. To our serendipitous advantage, HIAA (3.33 × 10−5 M) was also effective under the given conditions, and the luminescence quenching efficiency was 85 % [3] in the presence of HIAA (Fig. 3C and D). In sharp contrast, selected ions (3.33 × 10−5 M, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, Bu4NF, Bu4NCl, Bu4NBr, Bu4NI, Bu4NOAc, Bu4NOH, Bu4PF6, Bu4NBF4, Bu4NClO4, Bu4NH2PO4, Na2S, KCN, NaN3, K3PO4, K2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na3PO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaNO3, NaNO2, NaHSO4, Na2SO4, MgSO4, and NH4Cl), ROS (H2O2, ClO−, 1O2, KO2, tBuO., tBuOOH), bio-phosphates (ATP, ADP, AMP, GTP, GDP, GMP, and PPi), amino acids (GSH, l-proline, histidine, serine, homocysteine, lysine, isoleucine, cysteine, tyrosine, glycine, histamine) and biomolecules (i.e. sarcosine, creatine, creatinine, urea, uric acid, acetone, glucose, lactic acid, citric acid, ascorbic acid, folic acid and dopamine) did not alter the luminescence spectra of CDs (Fig. 3C and D). Considering the multitude and diversity of the analytes tested in here, the data unambiguously show that CDs induced specific response to 5-HT and HIAA among many biologically relevant species. Concurrently, we inferred that CDs were highly selective to 5-HT and HIAA.

Besides, we measured the concentration dependent response of CDs to 5-HT and HIAA in water. Fig. 4 A and B shows that incremental addition of 5-HT and HIAA quenches the luminescence of CDs. A gradual increase in concentrations of 5-HT and HIAA causes distinct reductions of the phosphorescence intensity of CDs in a concentration dependent manner. Notably, the effective concentrations of 5-HT and HIAA were 3.22 × 10−6 M and 3.22 × 10−6 M, respectively, leading to disappearance of the green emission of CDs when illuminated with UV light (365 nm). Epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine, and glutamate represent a series of neurotransmitters as potential interferents. Coexistence of these neurotransmitters may drastically reduce the selectivity of CDs. Therefore, we also performed additional experiments to reveal the effect of these critical neurotransmitters along with glucose and proline. As it can be deduced from Fig. 4C, none of these species did interfere with 5-HT or HIAA. Based on the luminescent titration curves, limit of detections (LODs) for 5-HT and HIAA were found to be 0.44 nM and 0.49 nM, respectively (Fig. S2). Remarkably, these are the lowest reported values for luminescent systems to date [3,10,26]. A comparison of LODs with literature values is given in Table S1 (see ESI). It should be noted that the critical concentrations of 5-HT and HIAA in carcinoid tumor patients are around 1.9 μM and 126 μM [3,10,26]. Therefore, CDs are enabled to detect the dangerous concentrations of these carcinoid tumor markers. Furthermore, it was noteworthy that there was a linear relationship between I0/I and concentrations of both 5-HT and HIAA (Fig. S3). Stern-Volmer constants (Ksv) for 5-HT and HIAA were calculated as 1.46 × 105 M−1 and 4.40 × 104 M−1, respectively. Note that this is the highest reported Ksv value for 5-HT to date among the luminescent systems [3,10,26]. Collectively, the results of luminescent titration experiments suggest that CDs exhibit superior features for the selective and sensitive luminescence detection of 5-HT and HIAA among many biologically relevant species.

Encouraging by the above results, we next evaluated the feasibility of CDs for the analysis of 5-HT with blood plasma components. It is well established that when carcinoid tumors release 5-HT, its level in blood plasma of cancer patients will be increased. Therefore, to show the premise of the CDs, they were tested against a panel of blood plasma components including NaCl, KCl, NaHCO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, glucose, urea, and l-proline in water (Fig. 5 A) [3,10]. Surprisingly, CDs were almost silent in the presence of these blood plasma components whereas 5-HT lead to quenching of the phosphorescence signal efficiently (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, we examined the effect of potential interferents under the same conditions. Notably, these interferents (NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, Bu4NF, Bu4NCl, Bu4NBr, Bu4NI, Bu4NOAc, Bu4NOH, Bu4PF6, Bu4NBF4, Bu4NClO4, Bu4NH2PO4, Na2S, KCN, NaN3, K3PO4, K2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na3PO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaNO3, NaNO2, NaHSO4, Na2SO4, MgSO4, NH4Cl), H2O2, ClO−, 1O2, KO2, tBuO., tBuOOH, ATP, ADP, AMP, GTP, GDP, GMP, PPi, GSH, l-proline, histidine, serine, homocysteine, lysine, isoleucine, cysteine, tyrosine, glycine, sarcosine, creatine, creatinine, urea, uric acid, acetone, glucose, lactic acid, citric acid, ascorbic acid, folic acid and dopamine) produced negligible changes in the luminescence spectra of CDs (Fig. 5A). To perceive the selectivity of CDs to 5-HT, competition experiments were also carried out. To this end, simulated blood plasma samples, consisting of a mixture of NaCl, KCl, NaHCO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, glucose, urea, and l-proline, were prepared according to literature procedures [3,10]. CDs were added to the solution of simulated blood plasma to obtain a final concentration of 32.3 μg/mL of CDs. Next, the response of CDs to 5-HT was recorded. CDs elicited selective response to 5-HT in simulated blood plasma (Fig. 5C). Clearly, the results strongly supported that CDs were capable of detecting 5-HT in simulated blood plasma samples. Eventually, the response of CDs in simulated blood plasma correlated with the concentration of 5-HT, as shown in Fig. 5D.

It is reported that when tumors release 5-HT, the level of its main metabolite HIAA will be increased in urine samples of the cancer patients. In this juncture, HIAA represents an important biomarker of carcinoid tumors. This motivated us to evaluate the potential of CDs as optical chemosensors for HIAA. Hence, we investigated the effect of representative components of urine to luminescence spectra of CDs. In this regard, NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, NH4Cl, glucose, creatine, creatinine and urea, which are the main components of urine [57] were tested with CDs. As manifested in Fig. 6 A and B, none of these species elicited a discernible change in the luminescence spectra of CDs. Conversely, HIAA revealed a significant decrease in the phosphorescence intensity of CDs. Once we confirmed the selective response of CDs to HIAA, we exploited the capability of CDs to recognize HIAA in synthetic urine samples. Synthetic urine samples were prepared as a mixture of NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, NH4Cl, glucose, creatine, creatinine and urea [57]. To perceive the selectivity of CDs to HIAA, synthetic urine was used as the solvent milieu for spectroscopic titrations at this stage. In essence, we noticed that CDs did not respond to other relevant species when synthetic urine was used as the solvent milieu (Fig. 6C). HIAA abolished the phosphorescence signal of CDs, presumably initiating a luminescence-quenching process (Fig. 6C). Notably the response of CDs was concentration dependent (Fig. 6D). All the above results indicated that CDs were promising as optical chemosensors for HIAA.

Next, we wanted to expand the versatility of CDs in other biological fluids. We surmised that CDs can also be used for the detection of 5-HT in artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF). It is reported that abnormal levels of 5-HT in the cerebrospinal fluid is associated with dysfunctional serotonergic neurons of the central nervous system [24]. Therefore, the detection of 5-HT in the cerebrospinal fluid is challenging. In this point of view, we have prepared the physiological ion solution of the artificial cerebrospinal fluid, which was consisted of NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, Na2HPO4, and NaH2PO4 [24]. CDs were added to this solution to evaluate their potential as chemosensor for 5-HT in the cerebrospinal fluid. Epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine, glucose and urea were also evaluated under the given conditions since they may co-exist with 5-HT in the cerebrospinal fluid [58]. Fig. 7 A and B demonstrate that CDs gave negligible response to epinephrine, norepinephrine, glutamate, glucose and urea. However, 5-HT induced remarkable changes in the luminescence spectrum of CDs. Also, significant luminescence quenching was also observed to an extent in the presence of dopamine (Fig. 7A and B). It seems that the response of CDs to dopamine is affected by the type of media or artificial biological fluids and their ingredients. Next, measured the concentration dependent response of CDs to 5-HT and dopamine. We have found that the concentrations of 5-HT and dopamine are well correlated with luminescence quenching efficiency (Fig. 7C and D). We believe that CDs were promising for the accurate detection of 5-HT and dopamine in the cerebrospinal fluid.

5-HT is stored by the vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT) proteins [7]. In fact, VMAT proteins can store high concentrations of 5-HT up to 50–270 mM within secretory vesicles of specialized cells. However, the pH of the secretory vesicles is around 5.0 [7]. Keeping these facts in mind, we also tested CDs for the recognition of 5-HT in the environment of secretory vesicles. Moreover, other neurotransmitters including epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine were also assessed in the spectrophotometric titrations mimicking the environment within secretory vesicles. Fig. 8 A and B presents the obtained data from the luminescence measurements conducted in the environment mimicking secretory vesicles. e have found that CDs could recognize 5-HT by inducing remarkable luminescence quenching (ca. 90 % quenching efficiency) under these conditions. As a bonus, it is noteworthy that the detection of dopamine was also feasible with CDs albeit with a lower quenching efficiency (ca. 50 % quenching efficiency) when compared to 5-HT. Fig. 8C and D depict the luminescence spectra versus concentrations of 5-HT and dopamine, respectively. As such, it is clear that CDs were suitable for the analysis of both 5-HT and dopamine in the environment within secretory vesicles. Consistently, CDs emerge as excellent chemosensor candidates for the detection of 5-HT in biological fluids as real-samples.

It should be noted that the intensity changes of emission band between 300 and 400 nm is a key to distinguish 5-HT from dopamine (see Fig. 7A and compare 7C with 7D). Consequently, we investigated ratiometric response of CDs to 5-HT and dopamine. Hence, luminescence responses of CDs at 344 nm and 544 nm, respectively, were examined as a function of 5-HT and dopamine concentrations. A linear relationship between luminescence intensity ratio (I344/I544) and 5-HT concentration in the range of 0.0–4.0 × 10-7 M (see Fig. 9 A and B, red circles) was observed. Therefore, we were able to design a ratiometric factor between luminescence responses of CDs to 5-HT. In sharp contrast, there was no linear relationship between luminescence intensity ratio (I344/I544) and dopamine concentration (see Fig. 9A and B, blue squares). Overall, we have successfully shown that CDs could be used as a ratiometric optical sensor to track and distinguish 5-HT from dopamine and other analytes.

To conclude, we disclose the synthesis and characterization of Tb(III)-doped CDs. It was noteworthy that CDs demonstrated excellent performance as chemosensors for 5-HT and HIAA. Remarkably, they CDs induced concurrent luminescent response to both 5-HT and HIAA in water. Limit of detections (LODs) for 5-HT and HIAA were found to be 0.44 nM and 0.49 nM, respectively. It is remarkable that the LODs are the lowest reported values for luminescent systems to date. It should be noted that the critical concentrations of 5-HT and HIAA in carcinoid tumor patients are around 1.9 μM and 126 μM. Therefore, CDs are enabled to detect the dangerous concentrations of these carcinoid tumor markers. Moreover, CDs were explored as optical chemosensors for 5-HT, and HIAA along with potential co-existing neurotransmitters in biological fluids. To accomplish this, photoluminescence titration experiments were performed with CDs against a panel of both simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine components. Strikingly, CDs induced highly selective response to 5-HT even in the presence of simulated blood plasma components. Furthermore, the determination of 5-HT was also viable when simulated blood plasma was used as the background in competition experiments. In a similar vein, we found that CDs were capable of recognizing HIAA selectively among synthetic urine components. We also achieved successful detection of HIAA in synthetic urine samples. Accordingly, the obtained data attested that CDs were highly promising for the detection of 5-HT and HIAA in simulated blood plasma and synthetic urine samples, respectively. More importantly, we noticed that individual or simultaneous detection of 5-HT and dopamine in artificial cerebrospinal fluid was feasible. Finally, we successfully show that the CDs can be used for concurrent detection of 5-HT and dopamine in the environment of secretory vesicles. Overall, the versatility of CDs as chemosensors for 5-HT, HIAA and dopamine was delicately presented by applications to biological fluids. To our best knowledge, this is the first example of CDs, which can induce remarkable luminescence response to 5-HT, HIAA and dopamine in biological fluids. Finally, we anticipate that CDs are unique chemosensors for tracking 5-HT and HIAA in real life samples for biomedical applications. Further work is currently underway in our laboratory and the results will be reported in due time.
